explanation: The phrase "while prop_1" suggests that prop_0 must be true whenever prop_1 is true. The "shall" implies that this must hold globally.

The natural language phrase "A while B" typically translates to "If B is true, then A is true." In LTL, this universal truth translates to a global implication: G (B -> A). Therefore, "The system shall provide means to prop_0 while prop_1" means that it must always be the case that if 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' is also true.

The phrase 'while prop_1' indicates that prop_0 must hold whenever prop_1 is true. This condition must be maintained globally throughout the execution. Therefore, if prop_1 is true at any point, prop_0 must also be true at that same point, expressed as a global implication.

The statement "The system shall provide means to prop_0 while prop_1" means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true. This must hold globally, so we use the G (globally) operator along with an implication.
The phrase "while prop_1" indicates that the condition for prop_0 is concurrent with prop_1. Therefore, if prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true. This is represented by a simple implication.

The phrase "while prop_1" indicates a condition that must hold for the primary action. This means that if 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' must also be true simultaneously. This is best represented as a global implication, stating that at all times, if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' must also hold.
The phrase "while prop_1" indicates a condition under which "prop_0" must hold. This translates to a global implication: whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true.
The phrase "while prop_1" indicates that prop_0 must be true whenever prop_1 is true. This translates to a global implication: if prop_1 is true, then prop_0 must be true.

The phrase 'while prop_1' indicates a continuous condition: whenever 'prop_1' is true, 'prop_0' must also be true. This translates to a global implication: it must always be the case that if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' also holds.

The phrase "while prop_1" indicates a condition under which "prop_0" must hold. This means that if "prop_1" is true at any given time, "prop_0" must also be true at that same time. The "shall" implies that this condition must hold globally. Therefore, the LTL formula expresses that it is globally true that if 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' must also be true.
